KUKWATAZIM ← Back to Site

Step 01 of 04 — The Warning

You were told
not to eat here.

Every Zimbabwean child knows this feeling. Mama said no. The neighbours said no. Curiosity said yes.

"Usadye pese pese..." — Don't eat everywhere, mwana.

Step 02 of 04 — Raise The Spoon

🥄

Raise it.
Hold it.

Above the head. Mock-serious expression. The dramatic pause that says: I know what I'm doing. I know this is questionable. I am doing it anyway.

One moment of stillness before the adventure.

Step 03 of 04 — Say The Word

NGATIKWATEYI.

Let's eat. Let's try it. Let's experience this together.
The one word that opens every table in Zimbabwe.

nga·ti·kwa·tey·i — "Let us eat together." An invitation, not just a meal.

Step 04 of 04 — The First Bite

🍽️

Now.
Eat.

The ritual is complete. Curiosity has won. The food is in front of you. The ancestors are watching — and they are, mostly, okay with this.

Ko kumba kunenge kuna sadza. But today — ngatikwateyi.

🥄 RITUAL
COMPLETE

First Bite
Unlocked.

You have performed the ritual. The spoon was raised. The word was spoken. The food awaits.

Ngatikwateyi. — You may now eat freely.

A Kukwatazim Cultural Tradition

The First
Bite
Ritual

Before every unfamiliar meal, every forbidden food,
every table you weren't supposed to eat at — you perform the ritual.

Tap the spoon. Perform the ritual. Eat freely.

🍽️
First Bite
The Cultural Meaning

What is the
Ritual?

The First Bite Ritual is Kukwatazim's signature cultural moment. It borrows from the Zimbabwean spirit of kukwata — the cheeky, curious act of eating at someone else's table when you weren't supposed to.

Before trying anything unfamiliar — a new food, a foreign restaurant, a dish that made Amai raise an eyebrow — you perform the ritual. It is playful protection. It is communal permission. It says: I know this is questionable. I'm doing it anyway. Ngatikwateyi.

  • 01

    The Warning — Face the doubt

    Acknowledge that you were told not to eat this. Mama said no. The neighbours said no. Curiosity says yes.

  • 02

    Raise the Spoon — The dramatic pause

    The wooden spoon goes up. The expression goes serious. One beat of stillness before the unknown.

  • 03

    Ngatikwateyi — The invitation

    Say the word. Invite the experience. Open the table to everyone around you.

  • 04

    The First Bite — Curiosity wins

    The ritual is complete. You eat. The ancestors are watching. They are mostly okay with this.

Ko kumba hakuna sadza here? Is there no sadza at home?
Usadye pese pese! Don't eat everywhere, mwana.
...okay. Fine. Eat. Reluctant approval. Classic Amai.
Community

Do the ritual.
Share the bite.

The First Bite Ritual only works when it's shared. Hover each food below and see if Amai approves — then try it yourself.

Rituals Performed in this session. Each one a small act of cultural curiosity.
🍲 ✓ Approved

Sadza & Nyama choma

Amai says: "Now THIS is food."

🍣 ~ Hmm...

Sushi in Harare

Amai says: "Raw fish? Ahh mwana wangu..."

🍔 ✗ Disowned

McDonald's

Amai says: "At home we have food."

🍜 ~ OK

Chinese food in Harare

Amai says: "People eat this willingly?"

🌮 ~ Maybe

Tacos

Amai says: "What even is this shape?"

🍖 ✓ Yes

Street braai

Amai says: "This is acceptable. Eat."
